rulings.Case Title: JOSE CERON VS. SALLY LIU ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Attorneys Fees
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 16, 2019 on Line 13, PLAINTIFF JOSE CERON, MONICA MEDINA, BRIAN MEDINA and JUDY JUDKINS' Motion For Attorneys Fees Plaintiffs' motion for award of attorney's fees and costs after appeal is granted in the amount of $25,340.00 in attorney's fees and $489.95 in costs. On November 25, 2015, judgment pursuant to a jury award was entered against defendant Sally Liu in the total amount of $48,967.00. On the same date, the Court granted plaintiffs' motion for attorney's fees and costs against defendant pursuant to San Francisco Rent Ordinance section 37.10B, which provides, "a prevailing party shall be entitled to reasonable attorney's fees and costs pursuant to order of the court." The award was in the amount of $22,750 for attorney's fees and costs in the amount of $1,847. The Court of Appeal affirmed the judgment (No. A147803, unpub. opn. filed Mar. 28, 2019) and denied rehearing, and the California Supreme Court denied review. (No. S255641, June 12, 2019.) The Court takes judicial notice of the Court of Appeal's opinion and the Supreme Court's order denying defendant's petition for review pursuant to Evid. Code § 451(a). Plaintiffs seek compensation for their efforts in (i) responding to defendant's unsuccessful order for order directing filing of satisfaction of judgment and return of over-collected amounts; (ii) preparing a respondents' brief in an appeal filed by defendant in which she represented herself in pro per; (iii) reviewing defendant's reply brief and unsuccessful petitions for rehearing and petition for review; and (iv) preparing the instant motion. Both parties' discussion of the facts of other cases in which defendant was involved is irrelevant and inappropriate; the sole question before this Court is whether the amount of attorney's fees sought by plaintiffs in the instant case is reasonable. Based on its review of the record and of counsel's time records, the Court finds that the amount of time for which counsel seeks fees, 90.5 hours, is excessive for the quantity and nature of the work performed, and that calculation of a reasonable lodestar requires a 20% reduction, to 72.4 hours. The Court further finds that $350 per hour is a reasonable hourly rate for counsel's time. Counsel does not seek a multiplier, nor does the Court believe that one is appropriate. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TOM BREEN VS. SPRINGLINE, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Lc General Engineering & Construction, Inc., Argonaut Insurance Company Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint (2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 16, 2019 on Line 4, Defendant Lc General Engineering & Construction, Inc., Argonaut Insurance Company Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint (2 Of 2) Finally, Plaintiff's request for leave to amend to state a cause of action for quantum meruit is denied. "[A]s a matter of law, a quasi-contract action for unjust enrichment does not lie where, as here, express binding agreements exist and define the parties' rights." (California Medical Association, Inc. v. Aetna US Healthcare of California, Inc. (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 151, 172 [trial court properly sustained without leave to amend demurrer to quasi-contract claim for reasonable value of services rendered by plaintiffs to third parties].) Plaintiff had an express contract with Springline. Any benefits conferred on LC General were incidental to its contractual obligation to Springline, and LC General therefore cannot be deemed to have been unjustly enriched. (See id. at 174 ["any benefit conferred upon defendants by Physicians was simply an incident to Physicians' performance of their own obligations to Intermediaries under the Intermediary-Physician Agreements."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TOM BREEN VS. SPRINGLINE, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Lc General Engineering Construction, Inc. And Argonaut Insurance Company'S Notice Of Motion To Dismiss Second Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 16, 2019 on Line 4, DEFENDANT LC GENERAL ENGINEERING & CONSTRUCTION, INC., ARGONAUT INSURANCE COMPANY Defendants Lc General Engineering Construction, Inc. And Argonaut Insurance Company'S Motion To Dismiss Second Amended Complaint Defendant LC General Engineering & Construction, Inc.'s motion to dismiss the second amended complaint for delay in prosecution is denied. The original complaint in this case was filed on September 6, 2016. Plaintiff served LC General with the complaint on September 30, 2016, LC General participated in discovery in this action, and its counsel met and conferred with plaintiff's counsel regarding the causes of action alleged in the original complaint. (Olsen Decl. 9-13 & Ex. C; Reply at 2 ["Breen did serve the complaint on LCG and respond to discovery"].) For reasons that are not clear to the Court, however, it appears that LC General never filed a responsive pleading to the complaint or the first amended complaint. Defendant Argonaut Insurance Company's motion to dismiss is granted. Argonaut is not named as a party in the second amended complaint (or prior complaints), and it appears that plaintiff never sought leave to amend the complaint to name Argonaut as a Doe defendant. Nevertheless, the court's docket reflects that plaintiff first purported to serve Argonaut with the summons and second amended complaint on July 10, 2019. The court has discretion to dismiss a complaint for delay in prosecution if service is not made within two years after the action is commenced against the defendant. (Code Civ. Proc. § 583.420(a)(1).) The period runs from the commencement of the action, which means when the original complaint is filed. (Code Civ. Proc. §§ 583.210(a), 411.10.) The rule applies even where the defendant seeking dismissal was served as a Doe defendant. (See Inversiones Papaluchi S.A.S. v. Superior Court (Robinson Helicopter Company, Inc.) 20 Cal.App.5th 1055, 1061 [cross-complaint dismissed where defendants not designated as Roes until almost three years later and not served until more than three years later; time for service ran from filing of original cross-complaint].) Here, defendants show that as early as January 2015, plaintiff was aware of Argonaut's identity as one of the two surety companies on the project. (Meyer Decl. 7.) Plaintiff concedes as much in his second amended complaint. (See SAC 35-36 [alleging that on January 23, 2015, Argonaut denied Breen's claim on payment bonds].) Moreover, plaintiff does not offer any excuse for his nearly three-year delay in serving Argonaut. "When the plaintiff has not served within the two-year period, the plaintiff is required to show excusable delay." (Terzian v. County of Ventura (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 78, 83; accord, Putnam v. Clague (1992) 3 Cal.App.4th 542, 549 ["'When faced with a motion to dismiss it is incumbent upon plaintiff to establish reasonable or excusable delay in service. If such a showing is not made, the court may dismiss the action in its discretion even if defendant fails to establish actual prejudice.'"].) Because plaintiff has not attempted to meet his burden, Argonaut's motion must be granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: TOM BREEN VS. SPRINGLINE, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 16, 2019 on Line 4, DEFENDANT LC GENERAL ENGINEERING & CONSTRUCTION, INC., ARGONAUT INSURANCE COMPANY DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT (1 of 2) Defendant LC General Engineering & Construction, Inc.'s demurrer to the second amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to the first and third causes of action, and is off calendar as moot with respect to the second cause of action. The demurrer by Argonaut Insurance Company is off calendar in light of the court's ruling granting Argonaut's motion to dismiss for delay in prosecution. The demurrer to the first cause of action for claim for payment on stop-notice release bond is sustained without leave to amend. A claimant "shall commence an action against the public entity and the direct contractor to enforce payment of the claim stated in a stop pay notice not later than 90 days after expiration of the time within which a stop payment notice must be given." (Civ. Code § 9502(b).) A stop payment notice must be given no later than 90 days after completion. (Civ. Code § 9356.) Here, Plaintiff concedes that certificates of completion were filed in the Public Works Projects on Oct. 16, 2014, and that he filed stop notices with the City on December 3, 2014. (2d Am. Compl. 26-27, 32; Def.'s Opp'n 4.) This action was first filed on Sept. 6, 2016, nearly two years after completion. Plaintiff's claim for payment on the stop-notice release bond is therefore time-barred. The demurrer to the second cause of action for bad faith denial of claim on surety bond is off calendar as moot. The demurrer to the third cause of action for account stated is sustained without leave to amend. "An account stated is an agreement, based on prior transactions between the parties, that all items of the account are true and that the balance struck is due and owing from one party to the other." (Trafton v. Youngblood (1968) 69 Cal.2d 17, 25; see also Gardner v. Watson (1915) 170 Cal. 570, 571 ["An account stated is a document, a writing, which exhibits the state of account between parties and the balance owing from one to the other."].) While the existence of an account stated may be implied from the circumstances, the essential elements of an account stated are "(1) previous transactions between the parties establishing the relationship of debtor and creditor; (2) an agreement between the parties, express or implied, on the amount due from the debtor to the creditor; (3) a promise by the debtor, express or implied, to pay the amount due." (Zinn v. Fred R. Bright Co. (1969) 271 Cal.App.2d 597, 600.) Here, Plaintiff has not alleged any facts that establish any of these essential elements. Rather, plaintiff alleges that LCG General was the general contractor on the three public works projects, but that it had an agreement with a subcontractor, Springline, to provide hauling services and equipment leasing services to Springline in connection with those projects. (SAC 12, 21, 85.) The mere fact that LC General may have been "aware of" the agreement between plaintiff and Springline or of plaintiff's work (Id. 22, 86) cannot give rise to an account stated. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: KEVIN M FRANKLIN VS. UNION AND POLK LP. A CALIFORNIA LIMITED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion 1 To Compel Plaintiff Keehae Park S Appearance For Deposition 2 To Compel Further Answers At Ms. Park S Deposition 3 To Limit Plaintiffs Counsel To Objections Based On Privilege And 4 For Monetary Sanctions Against Ms. Park And Her Counsel In The Amount Of $5,940
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 16, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANTS ADAMAS BUILDERS INC., TONY DIGIORNO, ADAMAS DEVELOPMENT, LLC, UNION AND POLK PARTNERS, LP, ANDREW BROUGHTON's Motion 1-To Compel Plaintiff Keehae Park's Appearance For Deposition, 2-To Compel Further Answers At Ms. Park's Deposition, 3-To Limit Plaintiff's Counsel To Objections Based On Privilege, And 4-For Monetary Sanctions Against Ms. Park And Her Counsel In The Amount Of $5,940. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel is GRANTED AND DENIED IN PART. Ms. Park must sit for a continued deposition. Date should be set by agreement: Defendants have not shown why it is "critical" for her deposition to conclude before the "series of depositions" starting 9/26 (MPA at 13). There is nothing improper about Ms. Park's use of an interpreter. Counsel may not instruct a witness to not answer unless based on privilege or other recognized basis, when the question calls for a legal contention, or the questions unreasonably annoy, embarrass, or oppress the deponent. CCP 20225.460 (privilege must be claimed) 2025.470 (permitting suspension of deposition to move for order to protect against bad faith or harassing conduct); Stewart v. Colonial Western Agency (2001) 87 Cal.App.4th 1006 (if questions are harassing, the party should suspend and seek protective order); Rifkind v. Sup. Ct. (1994) (legal contention questions may be inappropriate at deposition). Turning to the questions in the Separate Statement. 1/Q at 12:12-22 - objections sustained - asked and answered at 11:1-18. 2/Q at 29:7-13, 3/Q at 29:19-24; 4/Q at 31:17-32:6 - objections overruled - the word "allegedly," as used in these questions, is not so argumentative that the questions cannot fairly be answered. 4/Q at 31:17-32:6 - while compound is not an appropriate basis to instruct not to answer, the question is compound and confusing. 5/Q at 39:11-17 - motion to compel denied - at 39:18 Plaintiff answered despite the instruction not to answer. Defendants did not move to compel further answers to the questions about the consequences of perjury, so the Court will not order further testimony as urged in the Reply. Moreover, Ms. Park testified that she understood she could be punished if she "state[s] something [that] is not true." 13:21-23. Plaintiffs' request to appoint a discovery special master is denied. This request should be raised in a properly noticed CCP 639 motion and not in an opposition. The requests for sanctions by both parties are DENIED because the motion is granted in part and denied in part. [Continued to Part 2 of 2] =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: COMMUNITY TENANTS' ASSOCIATION ET AL VS. VALSTOCK MANAGEMENT COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Appointment Of Discovery Referee (Joinder)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 16, 2019, Line 5 DEFENDANTS STEPHEN LEE, BROADWELL LLC's Motion For Appointment Of Discovery Referee (Joinder). Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: See tentative ruling on main motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: COMMUNITY TENANTS' ASSOCIATION ET AL VS. VALSTOCK MANAGEMENT COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of All Named Defendants For Court Appointment Of Discovery Referee
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 16, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT 371 BROADWAY STREET, LLC, VALSTOCK MANAGEMENT COMPANY, VALSTOCK VENTURES, LLC's Motion Of All Named Defendants For Court Appointment Of Discovery Referee. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is DENIED. Plaintiffs' declarations establish an economic inability to pay. Unless Defendants agree to shoulder the entire cost of a discovery referee, or locate a referee willing to work pro bono, the Court cannot find that requiring Plaintiffs to pay referee fees would be fair and reasonable. CCP 639(b)(6) (court may not appoint a referee where a party establishes an economic inability to pay unless another party agrees to pay), 645.1 (any payment of referee fees must be fair and reasonable); Taggares v. Sup. Ct. (1998) 62 Cal.App.4th 94, 98 (trial court must "retain the discovery motions if one side cannot afford to pay anything and there are no available cost-free alternatives"). That Ropes & Gray, or other defense counsel, is providing pro bono representation does not mean that counsel's resources may be considered. Both CCP 639(d)(6)(B) and 645.1(b) provide that the court may only consider the resources of a party and not of counsel. Taggares, 62 Cal.App.4th at 103 (court may not require counsel to cover referee fees). If Defendants agree to shoulder the entire cost of a discovery referee, or locate one who will work pro bono, then the motion would be granted. Taggares, 62 Cal.App.4th at 105 (factors to consider when appointing referee). Based on my review of the motions heard by Judge Pro Tem Fleming, I agree that the volume of motions and issues, and materials submitted in relation to those motions, would be difficult for a judge pro tem to evaluate in the ordinary course and ordinary timeframe. It would promote judicial economy and consistency to have a referee familiar with the issues in the case. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: STEPHEN CAMPBELL ET AL VS. PACIFIC ARMS GOUGH STREET LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Documents, Set One From Defendant Pacific Arms Gough Street Llc;; Request For Sanctions Against Defendant And Defendants Attorney Of Record, Gregory De La Pena, In The Amount Of $1,167
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 116, 2019, Line 4, PLAINTIFFS STEPHEN CAMPBELL, MAXWELL MCINTIRE's Motion To Compel Further Responses To Plaintiffs Request For Documents, Set One From Defendant Pacific Arms Gough Street LLC; Request For Sanctions Against Defendant And Defendant's Attorney Of Record, Gregory De La Pena, In The Amount Of $1,167. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is DENIED as untimely. Brown Decl. Ex. Q demonstrates that Plaintiffs had until May 28, 2019, to move to compel. CCP 2031.310(c) requires the parties to agree to a "specific later date" and not open-ended extensions. Even if there were an open-ended extension, Defendant's response at O'Brien Amended Decl. Ex. A demonstrates a good faith attempt to meet and confer and it was unnecessary to bring this motion before exhausting that process. The requests for sanctions by both parties are DENIED. Plaintiff's request is denied because the motion is untimely and Plaintiffs understood counsel was working on updated responses. Defendants' request is denied because Defendants admit to deficiencies in the discovery responses. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: KHEHRA DENTAL CORPORATION, ET AL VS. DELTA DENTAL OF CALIFORNIA,
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Renewed Motion And Motion To Strike
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 16, 2019 on Line 8, DEFENDANT DELTA DENTAL OF CALIFORNIA Notice Of Renewed Motion And Motion To Strike Defendant Delta Dental of California's motion to strike is denied. Delta asks this court to take judicial notice of its Membership Action Policies and Procedures ("MAPP") Rules. Delta contends that they are part of the parties' agreement and are not reasonably subject to dispute. (Evid. Code, § 452(h); Delta's Request for Judicial Notice, 1:5-10.) However, the court cannot take judicial notice of a private contract where the parties dispute its terms or applicability. (See Gould v. Maryland Sound Industries, Inc. (1995) 31 Cal.App.4th 1137, 1145 ["we hold the existence of a contract between private parties cannot be established by judicial notice" under Evid. Code § 452(h)]; Fremont Indemnity Co. v. Fremont General Corp. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 97, 115 [improper to take judicial notice of letter agreement].) In Ingram v. Flippo (1999) 74 Cal.App.4th 1280, the plaintiff alleged that the district attorney published a letter and a media release stating inaccurately that school district board members had violated the Ralph M. Brown Act (Brown Act) (Gov. Code, § 54950 et seq.). The plaintiff sought declaratory and injunctive relief under the Brown Act. The complaint quoted from and summarized parts of the letter, the defendant filed a copy of the letter and media release in support of the demurrer, and both sides referred to the letter and quoted from it at the hearing on the demurrer. The defendants requested judicial notice of the letter and media release on appeal under Evidence Code section 452, subdivision (h), and the plaintiff did not oppose the request. Noting all of those circumstances, the Court of Appeal granted the request for judicial notice of the letter and media release. (Ingram, 74 Cal.App.4th at 1285, fn. 3.) No such circumstances exist in this case. Plaintiff's complaint does not summarize, attach, or quote the MAPP rules. Plaintiff opposes judicial notice of the MAPP rules and related documents and disputes whether they are applicable. Lastly, Delta concedes that the MAPP rules are merely referenced by implication in 24, 27, and 28 of the complaint. (Delta's Request for Judicial Notice, 1:25-2:3 and fn.1.) Judicial notice is not appropriate on this record. Pomona College v. Superior Court (1996) 45 Cal.App.4th 1716 does not compel a different result. There, the court considered evidence outside of the pleadings because the parties did not object. (Id. at 1721.) Plaintiff's complaint does not facially implicate the MAPP rules and therefore, the motion is denied. "The hearing on [a motion to strike] may not be turned into a contested evidentiary hearing through the guise of having the court take judicial notice of documents whose truthfulness or proper interpretation are disputable." Fremont Indemnity Co. v. Fremont General Corp. (2007) 148 Cal.App.4th 97, 114.) Based on the current record, Delta's internal review process and whether plaintiff must arbitrate her claims or exhaust her administrative remedies by seeking writ review per Code of Civ. Proc, 1094.5 or pursue some other remedy to insure a fair review process is unsettled. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: EDWARD COOPER VS. NICHOLAS SHER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 16, 2019 on Line 9, DEFENDANT NICHOLAS SHER's MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS (1 of 2) Defendant's motion for judgment on the pleadings is granted in full, with 20 days leave to amend. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 438.) The Court determines the meet and confer process was sufficient. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 439.) Defendant's main argument is that the alleged oral agreement is barred under the statute of frauds. (Code Civ. Proc., § 1624.) Plaintiff did not respond to this argument in its opposition. A contract that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making is invalid, unless in writing. (Code Civ. Proc., § 1624, subd. (a); see Lacy v. Bennett (1962) 207 Cal.App.2d 796, 809 ["The terms of the oral agreement may by express provision specify that the duty is not to be performed within a year, or by clear implication make it evident from the subject matter of the contract that a period longer than one year was contemplated by the parties."] The complaint does not provide sufficiently clear allegations for the court to determine whether the contract could be performed within a year or that the parties contemplated performance beyond one year. The complaint is vague and simply alleges the parties agreed to create GreenGenStorage "in pursuit of a green energy business opportunity." (Compl. 11; see Ladas v. California State Auto. Ass'n (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 761, 770; cf. Tostevin v. Douglas (1958) 160 Cal.App.2d 321 [it "was clear from the pleadings that payment was to continue so long as the program was broadcast throughout the world, the parties must have contemplated that the continued performance of the contract was to last more than one year from the date of making."].) Accordingly, the motion is denied on this ground, without prejudice to defendant raising the argument in response to an amended complaint containing a more specifically pled breach of contract claim. The motion is granted as to the first, second, and seventh causes of action because plaintiff does not allege sufficient facts to establish a valid contract for the reasons above. The plaintiff is granted leave to amend the complaint to allege with more specificity the terms of the alleged oral agreement. The motion is granted as to the third cause of action for breach of fiduciary duty, with leave to amend. According to the articles of organization, GreenGenStorage is a "manager-managed" LLC. Managers of an LLC owe the LLC and other members fiduciary duties such as duties of loyalty and care. (See Corp. Code, § 17704.09, subd. (f)(1).) In contrast, a member does not have a fiduciary duty to the LLC or another member solely by reason of being a member. (See Corp. Code, § 17704.09, subd. (f)(3).) However, there are no facts alleged as to whether defendant is a member or manager of GreenGenStorage, LLC. Nor, contrary to plaintiff's argument, does the complaint contain any allegation regarding the existence of an attorney-client relationship. Plaintiff fails to allege sufficient facts. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: EDWARD COOPER VS. NICHOLAS SHER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Nicholas Sher'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings (2 Of 2)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, September 16, 2019 on Line 9, Defendant Nicholas Sher'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings (2 Of 2) The motion is granted as to the fourth cause of action, negligence, with leave to amend. The complaint does not allege that defendant breached any duty independent of the contract. (See Ehrlich v. Menezes (1991) 21 Cal.4th 543, 551 ["Conduct amounting to a breach of contract becomes tortious only when it also violates a duty independent of the contract arising from principles of tort law."].) The complaint alleges only that defendant took steps in violation of the agreement which were not allowed in relation to the GreenGenStorage opportunity. (Compl. 13.) Plaintiff is correct that he is entitled to plead facts or legal theories in the alternative, or even inconsistently. (See Radar Co. v. Stone (1986) 178 Cal.App.3d 10, 29.) However, plaintiff has not sufficiently pleaded the necessary elements of negligence. The motion is granted as to the fifth cause of action, fraud, with leave to amend. Plaintiff's fraud claim fails because the allegations are vague and conclusory. (Compl. 32-37.) Plaintiff does not set forth the specific misrepresentations or omissions defendant allegedly made, that he justifiably relied on such misrepresentations, or that he suffered damages. (See Wilhelm v. Pray Price, Williams & Russell (1986) 186 Cal.App.3d 1324, 1331 [fraud claims require "justifiable reliance"]; Hall v. Department of Adoptions (1975) 47 Cal.App.3d 898, 904 [fraud claims must be pled with specificity].) The motion is granted as to the sixth cause of action, unjust enrichment, with leave to amend. California does not recognize a separate cause of action for unjust enrichment. (See McBride v. Boughton (2004) 123 Cal.App.4th 379, 387 ["Unjust enrichment is not a cause of action, however, or even a remedy, but rather a general principle, underlying various legal doctrines and remedies…we construe [plaintiff's] purported cause of action for unjust enrichment as an attempt to plead a cause of action giving rise to restitution."].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
